inference_unit.problem: Prior to this work there was an empirical and theoretical expectation, drawn from the families of layered copper-oxide superconductors, that the superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$ could increase when the number of CuO$_{2}$ planes per crystallographic unit cell is raised. A mercury-based compound, $HgBa_{2}CuO_{4+\delta}$, had been shown to superconduct at $T_{c}=94\,$K, but compounds in the $Hg$–$Ba$–$Ca$–$Cu$–$O$ (HBCCO) family with more than one CuO$_{2}$ layer per cell had not been demonstrated experimentally as superconductors with high $T_{c}$. Syntheses reported earlier (including attempts to make $Hg$-1212) either failed to produce superconductivity or produced poorly characterized multiphase material. As a consequence, it remained unresolved whether layered $Hg$-based multi-CuO$_{2}$ unit-cell compounds could realize $T_{c}$ values exceeding those known from thallium- and bismuth-based analogues, and there was no reliable structural, compositional and transport dataset linking specific HBCCO crystallographic phases to superconducting transitions above 100 K. This gap prevented: (a) an experimental test of the conjecture that $T_{c}$ rises with the number of CuO$_{2}$ planes in Hg-based systems; (b) determination of intrinsic $T_{c}$ values for pure HBCCO phases and their dependence on oxygen treatment; and (c) microscopic correlation between specific HBCCO unit cells (for example $Hg$-1212 versus $Hg$-1223) and measured magnetic and transport superconducting signatures. The resulting scientific consequence was that claims about record high $T_{c}$ in mercury-containing multi-layer cuprates lacked direct phase-resolved structural and compositional evidence, leaving unresolved whether observed high-temperature diamagnetism and resistive transitions originate from identified $HgBa_{2}Ca_{n-1}Cu_{n}O_{2n+2+\delta}$ phases or from minor, unidentified, or intergrown impurity phases.

conclusion.title: Magnetic detection of superconductivity with onset near 133.5 K and multi-phase magnetic signature

conclusions.conclusion:
The oxygen-annealed HBCCO specimen exhibits superconducting diamagnetism with an onset temperature $T_{c}\approx133.5\,$K measured in both zero-field-cooled (ZFC) and field-cooled (FC) SQUID magnetometry in an external field $H=27\,$Oe. The ZFC susceptibility at $T=6\,$K attains approximately $100\%$ of the ideal superconducting screening value $1/4\pi$ (assuming an average mass density $\rho\approx6~\mathrm{g\,cm^{-3}}$), indicating full magnetic screening of the sample at low temperature; the FC susceptibility reaches roughly $10\%$ of the maximum possible value at low temperature and attains $\sim60\%$ of its full low-temperature value by $T=125\,$K. The ZFC curve also shows additional diamagnetic features at $126\,$K and $112\,$K, indicating the presence of several distinct superconducting phases within the specimen.
In the study of high-$T_{c}$ copper-oxide superconductors, magnetic susceptibility measurements provide direct evidence of bulk superconductivity and screening. Prior work on mercury-containing cuprates had established superconductivity in single-layer $HgBa_{2}CuO_{4+\delta}$ at $94\,$K, but multi-layer mercury-based materials had not been unambiguously demonstrated as bulk superconductors. Because samples of putative multi-layer HBCCO compounds were often multiphase and of limited phase volume, magnetic screening signals could be diluted or confused with contributions from secondary phases. Consequently, before this work there was no clear magnetic evidence showing bulk superconductivity in Hg-based multi-CuO$_{2}$ materials at temperatures substantially above $100\,$K, nor a characterization of whether such superconductivity would appear as a single sharp transition or as multiple transitions from phase mixtures. Establishing a magnetic onset temperature and estimating the superconducting volume fraction were therefore essential to determine whether any observed high-temperature diamagnetism represents a bulk phase effect rather than a filamentary or impurity-related signal.
Fig. 2

conclusion.title: Electrical resistivity behavior: sharp drop near 132.5 K and zero resistance at 95 K; oxygen annealing raises onset temperature

conclusions.conclusion:
The four-probe resistivity $R(T)$ of an annealed HBCCO specimen shows a sharp drop in $R$ with a maximum in $dR/dT$ at $T\approx132.5\,$K and achieves exactly zero resistance within instrumental resolution at $T=95\,$K. Comparison with as-sintered samples shows that the final oxygen annealing increased the maximum observed $T_{c}$ from $\sim117\,$K (as-sintered) to the present onset near $132.5$–$133.5\,$K (oxygen-annealed).
Demonstrating superconductivity requires both magnetic and transport signatures; in layered cuprates the temperature where resistivity goes to zero and the onset of diamagnetism can differ due to phase inhomogeneity, percolation thresholds, or mixed-phase samples. Prior to this work there were no reported resistivity measurements for mercury-containing multi-layer HBCCO specimens that simultaneously displayed a high-temperature resistive anomaly (onset above $130\,$K) and a well-characterized zero-resistance state. Moreover, it was unclear whether oxygen treatment protocols effective in other cuprates would raise $T_{c}$ in mercury-based multi-layer materials. Therefore it was necessary to obtain temperature-dependent resistivity data for annealed samples to: (a) identify the temperature of the resistive transition and any broadness or multiple-step behavior indicative of phase mixtures; and (b) determine whether oxygen annealing could modify the superconducting onset temperature in HBCCO compounds.
Fig. 3

conclusion.title: Structural identification of Hg-1223, Hg-1212 and a long-period superstructure; lattice constants deduced

conclusions.conclusion:
High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and selected-area electron-diffraction (SAED) identify plate-like grains composed predominantly of $HgBa_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+x}$ (Hg-1223), disordered mixtures of Hg-1223 and $HgBa_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{6+x}$ (Hg-1212), and an ordered periodic stacking (superstructure) of these unit cells. From SAED patterns the tetragonal lattice constants are deduced as $c=12.7(2)\,\mathrm{\AA}$ for Hg-1212, $c=16.1(3)\,\mathrm{\AA}$ for Hg-1223, and $a=3.93(7)\,\mathrm{\AA}$ for both unit types. HRTEM images show an example stacking sequence 1223/1223/1212/1212/1223/1213 with a supercell $c\approx86.4\,\mathrm{\AA}$ extending beyond $2000\,\mathrm{\AA}$, establishing the superstructure as a distinct phase. No Hg-1201-type grains or HgO double layers were detected in the examined grains.
To attribute a high $T_{c}$ to a specific crystallographic HBCCO phase, high-resolution structural characterization is required to identify which unit-cell sequences or intergrowths are present and to measure their lattice parameters. Prior measurements in Hg-based systems were limited: successful syntheses of some Hg-phase compositions were reported without superconductivity, and earlier studies lacked high-resolution TEM/SAED evidence tying particular multi-layer Hg unit cells to superconducting behavior. Because multi-phase grain mixtures and long-period intergrowths can produce composite electronic responses, it was necessary to obtain direct SAED/HRTEM evidence of the presence, periodicity and lattice constants of candidate Hg-1212 and Hg-1223 unit cells and any superstructures, so that superconducting signatures could later be correlated with specific crystallographic motifs.
Fig. 1

conclusion.title: Sample phase composition determined by EDX and phase fractions

conclusions.conclusion:
Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrometry and bulk characterization indicate the annealed black material is a multiphase composite with approximate volume fractions: $\sim30\%$ $BaCuO_{2}$, $\approx30\%$ $CuO$, $\sim15\%$ of an unidentified oxide containing Hg, Ca and Cu, $<5\%$ of an oxide with Ca and Cu, $\sim5\%$ impurities of unspecified composition, and $\sim15\%$ of the total volume consisting of plate-like Hg–Ba–Ca–Cu-containing grains (the grains that HRTEM/SAED associate with Hg-1223/Hg-1212).
Reliable interpretation of magnetic and transport anomalies in newly synthesized cuprate samples requires knowledge of bulk phase composition: if the superconducting signal arises from a minor phase or from impurity phases, claims of high intrinsic $T_{c}$ for a targeted phase would be unsupported. Prior to this study, compositions of synthesized multi-layer Hg-containing samples were either not reported in detail or were ambiguous; therefore it was necessary to quantify the major phases and the volume fraction of Hg-containing grains to assess whether observed superconducting signatures could plausibly be bulk properties of Hg-based phases or instead originate from other components of the multiphase mixture.

conclusion.title: Suggested assignment of the $\sim$133 K superconducting phase to Hg-1223 (hypothesis, not proven)

conclusions.conclusion:
Based on the dominance of Hg-1223 observed in the grains investigated microscopically and the large relative superconducting volume fraction inferred near $125\,$K, it is suggested (without unambiguous proof) that $HgBa_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+x}$ (Hg-1223) is responsible for superconductivity with onset near $\sim133\,$K in these samples. This attribution is explicitly presented as a plausible interpretation rather than as a definitive identification.
Demonstrating which crystallographic phase produces a measured superconducting transition is fundamental for establishing materials chemistry–property relations in high-$T_{c}$ research. Before this work, there was no direct experimental link between multi-layer Hg-based unit cells and superconducting transitions above $100\,$K. The authors observed both a dominant presence of Hg-1223 in microscopic grain studies and a large superconducting fraction near $125\,$K; nonetheless, without single-phase material or phase-resolved measurements it remained possible that other intergrowths or minority phases produced the superconductivity. Thus a cautious, hypothesis-level assignment of the high-$T_{c}$ signal to Hg-1223 is the appropriate intermediate scientific claim pending phase-pure synthesis and definitive phase-to-property correlation experiments.
Fig. 1; Fig. 2

inference_unit.open_question: The study leaves several explicit unresolved scientific issues: (1) There is no unambiguous, phase-resolved proof that the observed superconductivity (onset near $133\,$K and resistive behavior) arises from members of the $HgBa_{2}Ca_{n-1}Cu_{n}O_{2n+2+\delta}$ family rather than from unidentified or minority impurity phases. The authors state they cannot presently relate the different superconducting transitions to specific crystallographic phases. (2) Precise lattice-parameter determination by X-ray methods was not possible because the volume fraction of the phases of interest is small; therefore accurate bulk crystallographic characterization of single-phase HBCCO materials remains to be achieved. (3) The disparity between the magnetically determined onset temperature ($\sim133.5\,$K) and the temperature of zero resistivity ($95\,$K) in the present multiphase specimens points to unresolved issues of phase inhomogeneity, percolation pathways or intergrain coupling that must be clarified by synthesis of larger-volume, single-phase material and by phase-resolved transport measurements. (4) Reproducible synthesis protocols that yield phase-pure Hg-1223 (or other specific HBCCO phases) with controlled oxygen stoichiometry, and subsequent definitive correlation between structure, composition and intrinsic $T_{c}$, are required to validate or refute the suggested assignment of the $\sim133\,$K superconductivity to Hg-1223.
